title: The Breath We Never Took
caption: On how the body keeps the ledger of early abandonment.
artworkAlt: A mature tree beneath a dramatic monochrome sky
body: There are some griefs that don’t arrive as memories but as symptoms. A tightening in the chest when love gets too close. A tumultuous stomach as weather before a storm, and a breath that never quite reaches the depth of your lungs. I’ve often felt that adoption writes itself into the body long before we ever learn the language to describe it. Not metaphorically. Literally. As if relinquishment isn’t only an emotional event but a physiological one. As if the body remembers what the mind was too young to record.
fullBody:
There are some griefs that don’t arrive as memories but as symptoms. A tightening in the chest when love gets too close. A tumultuous stomach as weather before a storm, and a breath that never quite reaches the depth of your lungs.

I’ve often felt that adoption writes itself into the body long before we ever learn the language to describe it.

Not metaphorically.
Literally.

As if relinquishment isn’t only an emotional event but a physiological one. As if the body remembers what the mind was too young to record.

I’ve never been able to breathe deeply. Not really. Even as a child I remember the strange frustration of it. Other kids seemed able to fill themselves effortlessly with air, expanding like sails catching wind. I breathed like someone borrowing oxygen from the world instead of belonging to it. Short breaths. Shallow breaths. Careful breaths.

When I played sports, I could feel it. Not just exhaustion, but restriction. Like my body was guarding itself from something invisible. As though somewhere beneath my ribs there was an old hand pressed gently but firmly against my sternum saying, Not too much. Don’t open fully. Don’t trust the world enough to inhale it.

And maybe that sounds poetic. Maybe it sounds dramatic. But I’ve heard enough adoptees describe the same sensation to wonder whether this isn’t coincidence at all. Maybe the body never forgets the first abandonment.

Science has begun to acknowledge what trauma survivors have always known. The body keeps score. Trauma doesn’t simply live in recollection. It lives in nervous systems. In cortisol levels. In hypervigilance. In digestion. In inflammation. In sleep. In breathing patterns.

So what happens when the trauma occurs before language? Before cognition? Before identity itself What happens when the rupture occurs at the precise moment the nervous system is first learning whether the world is safe?

For most infants, the mother is the first universe. Her heartbeat becomes weather. Her voice becomes geography. Her scent becomes home itself. Before we understand love intellectually, we understand it biologically. We’re regulated through proximity. Through touch. Through the rhythmic choreography of feeding, holding, soothing, breathing.

Then imagine that universe disappearing.

Not because the infant understands the social context of adoption. Not because blame exists. But because separation exists.

And separation, at its core, is physiological.

I sometimes think adoptees spend the rest of their lives trying to complete an interrupted inhale.

Not just emotionally.
Cellularly.

There’s something haunting to me about the vagus nerve. The great wandering nerve. The invisible river running from brainstem to abdomen, threading itself through the organs like a line stitched through fabric. It regulates rest, digestion, calm, emotional safety. It’s deeply connected to breath.

Deep breathing stimulates the vagus nerve. It tells the body it’s safe enough to soften. But what if your body learned the opposite at the beginning? What if your earliest lesson was that attachment disappears?

Perhaps that’s why so many adoptees seem suspended between yearning and vigilance. Between wanting intimacy and fearing annihilation. The nervous system becomes a smoke alarm that never stops listening for fire.

And the body adapts. Of course it adapts. Children always adapt. The tragedy is that adaptation often becomes identity.

The shallow breath becomes normal.
The tight stomach becomes personality.
The hyper-independence becomes maturity.
The emotional scanning becomes empathy.
The inability to fully rest becomes ambition.

People praise survivors for surviving without noticing survival itself can become a prison.

I’ve also noticed how many adoptees speak about stomach issues. Digestive problems. IBS. Chronic tension held in the abdomen like a fist that never unclenches. Anxiety that lives below the ribs. A constant feeling of unease with no obvious source.

And I can’t help but wonder whether there’s symbolism hidden inside the biology.

The abdomen is where our first connection existed.

The umbilical cord wasn’t just a medical structure. It was dependency made visible. Nourishment made physical. The first proof that we belonged to someone before we belonged to ourselves.

Then one day it was cut.

Every human being experiences that severance physically through birth, but adoption can create a second severance. One that arrives not through biology but through absence.

Perhaps the body doesn’t distinguish between metaphor and memory as cleanly as we do. Perhaps the belly remembers what the mind cannot articulate.

I think about trees sometimes.

How arborists can read the history of trauma in the rings hidden inside the trunk. Drought leaves a mark. Fire leaves a mark. Lightning leaves a mark. The tree continues growing around the wound, but the wound becomes part of the architecture.

Maybe adoptees grow like that.

Maybe we become beautiful despite the rupture, but also because we spent our lives growing around it.

There’s a particular loneliness in carrying a wound that society insists should only be discussed as gratitude.

Adoption narratives are often framed through rescue, salvation, fortune. We’re expected to speak in the language of blessings while quietly metabolising the language of loss.

But the body refuses propaganda.

The nervous system doesn’t care about social etiquette.

A child can be deeply loved by adoptive parents and still carry the imprint of separation. Those truths aren’t enemies. They can coexist painfully and honestly together.

Love after rupture doesn’t erase rupture.

Sometimes I wonder how many adoptees are walking around believing their bodies are faulty when in reality their bodies are speaking perfectly coherent grief.

What if anxiety isn’t always pathology but memory What if hypervigilance is the nervous system’s attempt to prevent another disappearance? What if the body stays partially clenched because somewhere deep within us exists the ancient understanding that people leave?

And what if healing isn’t about becoming “normal” but finally teaching the body that the story changed?

Maybe that’s why breathwork affects some adoptees so profoundly. It isn’t just relaxation. It’s renegotiation. Each deep breath becomes a conversation with the nervous system. A quiet insistence that says, You survived the severance. You can stop bracing now.

Though even writing that feels too simple. Because some wounds don’t vanish. They echo.

I’m not interested in pretending healing is linear or complete. I think some grief becomes part of our ecosystem. It changes shape over time but never entirely leaves. Like scar tissue beneath skin, invisible to strangers but felt whenever rain approaches.

Still, there’s something sacred in naming it.

In saying perhaps the body has been trying to tell the truth all along.

Perhaps adoptees aren’t weak because we struggle with regulation, trust, anxiety, intimacy, or chronic physical tension. Perhaps we are bodies that adapted brilliantly to impossible beginnings.

A body that learned to survive separation before it understood language deserves compassion, not judgment.

Sometimes I place my hand over my chest and try to breathe deeper than feels natural. Not performatively. Not spiritually. Just gently. Patiently.

As though I’m trying to convince some frightened animal within me that oxygen isn’t scarce anymore.

Sometimes it works.

Sometimes my lungs open for a few miraculous seconds and I feel grief rush in behind the air, as though sorrow itself had been waiting at the threshold all these years.

And perhaps that’s the hidden terror of deep breathing for some adoptees. To breathe fully may also mean to feel fully. To unclench may mean to remember.

Because what if the shallow breath was never failure at all? What if it was protection? What if somewhere inside the adoptee body exists an ancient intelligence whispering:

If you never inhale completely, maybe loss can never completely enter you again.

But I wonder too whether healing begins the moment we risk the deeper breath anyway.

Not because it erases what happened or reunion fixes it. Not that love rewrites the nervous system overnight or understanding suddenly untangles decades of embodied grief.

But because every full breath becomes an act of defiance against the original rupture.

A declaration that says:

I’m still here.

Perhaps the adoptee body never stops searching for the breath it lost when separation entered the room.
beforeReading: This reflection begins with a personal bodily experience and moves through questions about early separation, nervous-system memory, and adaptation. It isn’t a clinical account or a claim that every adoptee experiences the body in the same way. It is one person’s attempt to listen to what his own body may have been carrying.
originTitle: Listening to the body without making it a verdict.
origin: I began with a physical fact I had treated as a defect: I’ve never been able to breathe deeply. The piece grew from wondering whether that restriction belonged only to my lungs, or whether my body had been keeping an older record of separation than my conscious memory could reach. It came into sharper focus when other adoptees described sensations and adaptations that felt close to my own. I wanted to place those recognitions beside one another without turning them into a diagnosis.
meaningTitle: The body may be witness before it is symptom.
meaning: This piece marks a turn in my writing from asking what I remember to asking what remembers me. It helped me see that survival can hide inside habits praised as character: independence, alertness, endurance, the ability to read a room. The point isn’t that every breath has a single cause. It is that the body deserves to be treated as a witness rather than an inconvenience. Love after relinquishment may be real and sustaining, but it cannot travel backwards in time and prevent the first separation from having happened.
recognitionTitle: The piece returned through other bodies.
recognitionIntro: Selected responses from adoptee readers when this reflection was first shared.
seoTitle: The Breath We Never Took | Jonathan Lyon
seoDescription: Jonathan Lyon reflects on shallow breath, somatic memory, relinquishment, and the protective intelligence of the adoptee body.
